question: Open-source-friendly hardware for a TRUE wireless mesh (802.11s/IBSS) OpenWrt deployment: confirmed mainline support, strong mt76 or ath9k/ath10k drivers, >=2 radios, compact, external antenna connectors, bare-board form factor.
executive_summary: The prior failure was the driver, not the board: mt76 (mt7915/mt7916/mt7986) and ath9k genuinely support 802.11s/IBSS/SAE mesh in current OpenWrt, ath10k works with non-CT firmware, and ath11k/ath12k/wifi-qcom remain unreliable for mesh into 2026 (confirming the MikroTik/Qualcomm pain). No board is simultaneously fully-open, current-silicon, and multi-independent-radio, so one axis is traded. Recommended default is the Banana Pi BPI-R3 (mt76, bare board, U.FL, onboard 2.4+5 plus mPCIe/M.2 for a third radio); OpenWrt One ($89) is the lowest-risk board to validate the mesh stack first; BPI-R4 + 2x AsiaRF AW7916 cards gives true dedicated radios; Compex WPJ563/WLE200NX (ath9k/ath10k) is the max-reliability WiFi4/5 path; LibreRouter LR1 is the open-hardware purist option but hard to buy.

key_findings.finding: mt76 (mt7915/mt7916/mt7986 Filogic) and ath9k both support 802.11s mesh point, IBSS, and SAE-encrypted mesh in current OpenWrt; historical mt7915 5GHz mesh bugs were fixed 2022-2024.

ath11k/ath12k/wifi-qcom advertise mesh capability but are unreliable in mainline (STA/peer code paths crash, 2025-2026 issues); avoid for mesh. This corroborates the user's prior MikroTik/Qualcomm failure.
ath10k mesh works only with non-CT driver/firmware; avoid ath10k-ct. ath9k is the gold-standard mesh radio (AREDN standard) with no binary blob.

A single dual-band chip (MT7976) presents as two independent OpenWrt radios (2.4 + 5 GHz on separate phys), satisfying the dual-radio minimum; a dedicated 5GHz backhaul + 5GHz client (tri-radio ideal) needs an added mPCIe/M.2 card or a two-chip board.

Recommended modern bare boards: Banana Pi BPI-R3 (~$120, U.FL, mPCIe+M.2), OpenWrt One ($89, 3x MMCX, published schematics, first-class support), BPI-R4 + 2x AsiaRF AW7916 mPCIe cards for two independent radios. BPI-R3 Mini (65x65mm) is the most compact.

Max-reliability Atheros path: Compex WPJ563 board + WLE600VX (ath9k 2.4 + ath10k 5GHz, in production) or any mPCIe SBC + Compex WLE200NX (ath9k, no blob). PC Engines APU is EOL/NOS; Noah4C is the successor.

Operational rules: install wpad-mesh-mbedtls for SAE; target 802.11s not IBSS; keep backhaul on a fixed non-DFS 5GHz channel (avoid 160MHz/6GHz/DFS); use a dedicated backhaul radio (mesh+AP on one radio is fragile); L3/babeld rides over 802.11s as an L2 switch.

Packaging: route fragile U.FL (~30 cycles) to RP-SMA/SMA bulkheads (~500 cycles); directional/sector antenna on backhaul, omni on clients; PoE 802.3at or 12-24V DC; IP66-68 polycarbonate enclosure with Gore vent, conductive thermal path, conformal coating, Ethernet surge protection.

open_questions: Per-board 5GHz 802.11s mesh-point reliability at the specific OpenWrt snapshot to be flashed (mt76 #912 5GHz AP regression open, updated Feb 2026) - requires bench testing.
BPI-R4 dual-mPCIe-card simultaneous enumeration + power/thermal stability for two mt7916 cards - unverified.
Per-mPCIe-slot 3.3V current budget (4T4R mt76 cards want 2.5-3A) on BPI-R4/NanoPi - undocumented.
Whether ath10k Wave-1 (QCA9882) still needs the CT->non-CT swap on current OpenWrt 24.x.
OpenWrt One open-hardware completeness (Gerbers/CAD + license) and whether its M.2 slot is PCIe-capable for a WiFi NIC (second independent radio).
contradictions: H1 framed single-chip dual-band boards as not meeting '>=2 radios'; reconciled in synthesis section 3 - a dual-band chip presents as two radios on separate bands and does meet the dual-radio minimum; the tri-radio ideal needs an added card.
